cl_2023_pc_6_q_1.instructions:
Directions for questions 1 to 4: Study the following information and answer the questions that follow:

There are records that date back to 400 BC in which Socrates complains that it was better back in his day before everyone started learning how to write: “…this discovery of yours [writing] will create forgetfulness in the learners’ souls because they will not use their memories; they will trust to the external written characters and not remember of themselves.” Socrates feared that we would come to rely too heavily on the written word and, as a result, our memorization skills would fade. His fears weren’t unfounded — we have lost a degree of our mental acuity. And in recent years, it’s gotten worse. With Google, we’ve eliminated the need to memorize simple facts like names, dates, or basic equations. What good is memorizing the Pythagorean Theorem or the dates of the Civil War when that information is never more than a keystroke away? . . . .

Automation is making us dumber. We’re losing skills we once had. We’re not as good at flying planes, remembering dates, or talking on the phone. But it’s no different than Socrates’ 2500-year-old luddite argument — writing is making us worse at memorization. The only difference is that over the last 2500 years, we’ve come around to writing. We’ve collectively agreed that writing has been a net positive — we’ve used it to produce countless literary masterpieces, we’re able to communicate more efficiently, and you’re able to read my rants anywhere in the world! . . . Eventually, we’ll come around on automation and texting too. Because what’s valuable to one generation is not necessarily valuable to future generations. . . .

In 10,000 BC, we became worse at hunting because we discovered agriculture. In 1917, we became worse at hand-washing clothes because the washing machine took over. We’ve all made peace with all that. Why? Because when machines wash our clothes, when only 3% of the population can sustain an entire country with their crops, the rest of us have the time to pursue our own passions. When we don’t spend every waking minute trying to find our next meal or a safe place to sleep, we have the freedom to be creative. Like Maslow’s Hierarchy of Needs, the less we have to worry about, the better (and more creative and more fulfilling) our lives will be.

Over the course of history, technological developments that save us time, effort, energy, and brain power make humanity better off. Automation removes the need to sit on an assembly line and tighten bolts all day. Sure, the bolt-tightener loses his job, but the next generation of would-be bolt-tighteners go on to have more creative, more fulfilling jobs and as a result, better lives. In the next 10 years, a lot of jobs are going to be replaced by machines. Some skills will fade into obscurity. We’ll whine and complain and stomp our feet and say, “things were better back in the day.” And then we’ll all move on to complaining about the next thing…because, like it or not, that’s one of humanity’s greatest skills, and it will never fade.
cl_2023_pc_6_q_1.question_text: None of the following statements is implied by the arguments of the passage, EXCEPT:
options: Socrates opposed the use of writing and saw no value in it to preserve knowledge
Technology has eliminated the need for the individuals to think in critical terms.
Memorization skills declined in importance as information got readily available
People in the past were better at hunting animals in comparison to agriculture
cl_2023_pc_6_q_1.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
Your Answer : 3
The passage argues that with the advent of technologies like Google, we have eliminated the need to memorize simple facts like names, dates, or equations. The passage implies that memorization skills have declined in importance as we can access the information we need with a quick search instead of relying on our memories. The author also makes a comparison to Socrates' argument that writing was making us worse at memorization by highlighting that we have come to appreciate writing as a net positive despite the initial fears.

Wrong Answers:
1) This inference is incorrect because Socrates did not oppose the use of writing itself, but was rather concerned with how written communication would affect human memory. While he feared that the reliance on written language would lead to forgetfulness of memorized information, he also recognized the benefits of written communication for preserving knowledge and communicating ideas across time and space.
2) This inference is incorrect because technology has not eliminated the need for critical thinking or independent learning, but rather has changed how we access and process information. Memorization of basic facts is important, but critical thinking and the ability to understand and apply these facts is equally important in today's society. Technology can facilitate independent learning by providing access to online resources and forums.
4) Option 4 is not a valid inference because the passage does not make any claim or comparison about the relative hunting skills of people in the past versus agriculture. The author simply uses this example to illustrate how technological developments that save us time, effort, energy, and brain power make humanity better off.

cl_2023_pc_6_q_3.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
Your Answer : 4
This option is correct because the author implies that writing was not always seen as a positive and beneficial invention, but rather faced some resistance and criticism from people like Socrates, who argued that writing was making us worse at memorization. However, over time, most people have changed their views and attitudes towards writing, and have recognized its advantages and benefits for humanity.

Wrong Answers:
1) This option is incorrect because the author does not present writing as a preference, but rather as a historical development that influenced human culture and cognition. The author does not mention any individual goals or preferences that influenced the adoption or rejection of writing, but rather states that writing has affected our memory and mental acuity.
2) This option is incorrect because the author does not suggest that writing adhered to a divisive order that disrupted the existing order and norms, but rather that writing was a beneficial invention that enabled communication and learning. The author does not mention any conflicts or disagreements among different groups over the use or value of writing, but rather states that we have used it to produce literary masterpieces and access information.
4) This option is incorrect because the author does not claim that writing was an objective necessity that followed from logical and rational principles, but rather that writing was a controversial innovation that faced some opposition and criticism. The author does not appeal to any logical or rational principles to justify or explain the invention of writing, but rather compares it to automation and texting, which are also seen as making us dumber by some people.

The passage suggests that texting will also be accepted and appreciated by future generations, who will value it differently than the current generation.

Wrong Answers:
The other options are incorrect because they are either contradicted, exaggerated, or unsupported by the passage.

cl_2023_pc_6_q_5.instructions:
Directions for questions 5 to 8: Study the following information and answer the questions that follow:

For the vast majority of human history, the only concepts that could be understood were those of conscious thought and intentional behaviour. In the 1800s, two very different developments—hypnotism and evolutionary theory—pointed to the possibility of unconscious and unintended causes of human behaviour. One of these was hypnotism. The other was evolutionary theory. It hasn't helped that our view of the powers of the unconscious mind has come largely from studies of subliminal information processing, but modern psychological science is still wed to a conscious-centric model of the higher mental processes. This has been the case for nearly two centuries after the field of psychology was founded. This research, with its operational definition of the unconscious as a system that handles subliminal-strength stimulation from the environment, has helped to perpetuate the notion that conscious processes are primary and that they are the causal force behind most, if not all, human judgement and behaviour. Specifically, this research has contributed to the perpetuation of the notion that the unconscious is a system that handles subliminal-strength stimulation from the environment (e.g., Locke & Latham, 2002).

We propose an alternative viewpoint, according to which unconscious processes are defined in terms of their non-intentional nature, and the inherent lack of awareness is of the influence and effect of the triggering stimuli rather than of the triggering stimuli themselves (because nearly all naturally occurring stimuli are supraliminal). According to this definition of the unconscious, which is the first and most traditional one, recent research on priming and automaticity effects in the field of social cognition has demonstrated the existence of sophisticated, flexible, and adaptable behaviour guidance systems that are unconscious. It would appear that these have a high level of functional value, particularly as default behavioural tendencies that kick in whenever the conscious mind, as is its wont, travels away from the present environment into the past or the future. When the proprietor is not around, it is comforting to know that the unconscious is taking care of business.

In contrast to the other natural sciences, particularly neurobiology, the assumption of conscious primacy does not appear nearly as frequently as it does in the field of psychology. It is generally accepted that complex and intelligent design in living things is not the result of conscious processes on the part of the plant or animal, but rather the result of mindless adaptive processes that have accumulated as a result of natural selection (Dennett, 1995). This is not to say that human consciousness does not play a role or that it is not unique in its capabilities to transform, manipulate, and convey information in comparison to the mental powers of other animals; however, this consciousness is not required to achieve the sophisticated, adaptive, and intelligent behavioural guidance that is demonstrated in the emerging priming literature. According to Dawkins (1976), unconscious processes are intelligent and adaptive throughout the living world. The psychological research evidence that has emerged since the time that he wrote has confirmed that this principle applies to humans as well. The "unconscious mind" is the norm rather than the exception in the natural world.
cl_2023_pc_6_q_5.question_text: All of the following statements can be inferred by the arguments of the passage, EXCEPT:
options: Subliminal stimuli can influence human behaviour without conscious awareness.
The unconscious mind handles more than subliminal-strength stimulation from environment
Consciousness does not appear as a primary or essential feature in the subject of neurobiology
In the world of nature, the unconscious mind is the standard instead of being the anomaly.
cl_2023_pc_6_q_5.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2
Your Answer : 2
The unconscious mind handles more than just subliminal-strength stimulation from the environment. This is incorrect because the passage does not provide any evidence or example of other types or sources of stimulation that the unconscious mind handles. It only states that the unconscious is a system that handles subliminal-strength stimulation from the environment.

Wrong Answers:
1) Subliminal stimuli can influence human behaviour without conscious awareness. This is an inference that can be drawn from the passage, as it states that subliminal stimuli activate specific regions of the brain and can bias acting decisions even when they believe they are making free choices.
3) The passage states. “The assumption of conscious primacy does not appear nearly as frequently as it does in the field of psychology”. From that it means that, Consciousness does not appear as a primary or essential feature in the subject of neurobiology.
4) The last line of the passage tells that the "unconscious mind" is the norm rather than the exception in the natural world. From here the option can be inferred

Directions for questions 5 to 8: Study the following information and answer the questions that follow:

For the vast majority of human history, the only concepts that could be understood were those of conscious thought and intentional behaviour. In the 1800s, two very different developments—hypnotism and evolutionary theory—pointed to the possibility of unconscious and unintended causes of human behaviour. One of these was hypnotism. The other was evolutionary theory. It hasn't helped that our view of the powers of the unconscious mind has come largely from studies of subliminal information processing, but modern psychological science is still wed to a conscious-centric model of the higher mental processes. This has been the case for nearly two centuries after the field of psychology was founded. This research, with its operational definition of the unconscious as a system that handles subliminal-strength stimulation from the environment, has helped to perpetuate the notion that conscious processes are primary and that they are the causal force behind most, if not all, human judgement and behaviour. Specifically, this research has contributed to the perpetuation of the notion that the unconscious is a system that handles subliminal-strength stimulation from the environment (e.g., Locke & Latham, 2002).

We propose an alternative viewpoint, according to which unconscious processes are defined in terms of their non-intentional nature, and the inherent lack of awareness is of the influence and effect of the triggering stimuli rather than of the triggering stimuli themselves (because nearly all naturally occurring stimuli are supraliminal). According to this definition of the unconscious, which is the first and most traditional one, recent research on priming and automaticity effects in the field of social cognition has demonstrated the existence of sophisticated, flexible, and adaptable behaviour guidance systems that are unconscious. It would appear that these have a high level of functional value, particularly as default behavioural tendencies that kick in whenever the conscious mind, as is its wont, travels away from the present environment into the past or the future. When the proprietor is not around, it is comforting to know that the unconscious is taking care of business.

In contrast to the other natural sciences, particularly neurobiology, the assumption of conscious primacy does not appear nearly as frequently as it does in the field of psychology. It is generally accepted that complex and intelligent design in living things is not the result of conscious processes on the part of the plant or animal, but rather the result of mindless adaptive processes that have accumulated as a result of natural selection (Dennett, 1995). This is not to say that human consciousness does not play a role or that it is not unique in its capabilities to transform, manipulate, and convey information in comparison to the mental powers of other animals; however, this consciousness is not required to achieve the sophisticated, adaptive, and intelligent behavioural guidance that is demonstrated in the emerging priming literature. According to Dawkins (1976), unconscious processes are intelligent and adaptive throughout the living world. The psychological research evidence that has emerged since the time that he wrote has confirmed that this principle applies to humans as well. The "unconscious mind" is the norm rather than the exception in the natural world.

cl_2023_pc_6_q_9.instructions:
Directions for questions 9 to 12: Study the following information and answer the questions that follow:

Strangely, although we feel as if we sweep through time on the knife-edge between the fixed past and the open future, that edge — the present — appears nowhere in the existing laws of physics.

In Albert Einstein’s theory of relativity, for example, time is woven together with the three dimensions of space, forming a bendy, four-dimensional space-time continuum — a “block universe” encompassing the entire past, present and future. Einstein’s equations portray everything in the block universe as decided from the beginning; the initial conditions of the cosmos determine what comes later, and surprises do not occur — they only seem to. “For us believing physicists,” Einstein wrote in 1955, weeks before his death, “the distinction between past, present and future is only a stubbornly persistent illusion.”. . .

Physicists who think carefully about time point to troubles posed by quantum mechanics, the laws describing the probabilistic behaviour of particles. At the quantum scale, irreversible changes occur that distinguish the past from the future: A particle maintains simultaneous quantum states until you measure it, at which point the particle adopts one of the states. Mysteriously, individual measurement outcomes are random and unpredictable, even as particle behaviour collectively follows statistical patterns. This apparent inconsistency between the nature of time in quantum mechanics and the way it functions in relativity has created uncertainty and confusion.

Over the past year, the Swiss physicist Nicolas Gisin has published four papers that attempt to dispel the fog surrounding time in physics. As Gisin sees it, the problem all along has been mathematical. Gisin argues that time in general and the time we call the present are easily expressed in a century-old mathematical language called intuitionist mathematics, which rejects the existence of numbers with infinitely many digits. When intuitionist math is used to describe the evolution of physical systems, it makes clear, according to Gisin, that “time really passes and new information is created.” Moreover, with this formalism, the strict determinism implied by Einstein’s equations gives way to a quantum-like unpredictability. If numbers are finite and limited in their precision, then nature itself is inherently imprecise and thus unpredictable.

Physicists are still digesting Gisin’s work — it’s not often that someone tries to reformulate the laws of physics in a new mathematical language — but many of those who have engaged with his arguments think they could potentially bridge the conceptual divide between the determinism of general relativity and the inherent randomness at the quantum scale.

“I found it intriguing,” said Nicole Yunger Halpern, a quantum information scientist at Harvard University, responding to Gisin’s recent article in Nature Physics. “I’m open to giving intuitionist mathematics a shot.”

Cortês called Gisin’s approach “extremely interesting” and “shocking and provocative” in its implications. “It’s really a very interesting formalism that is addressing this problem of finite precision in nature,” she said.

Gisin said it’s important to formulate laws of physics that cast the future as open and the present as very real, because that’s what we experience. “I am a physicist who has my feet on the ground,” he said. “Time passes; we all know that.”
cl_2023_pc_6_q_9.question_text: None of the following statements is implied by the arguments of the passage, EXCEPT:
options: As per Einstein, believing physicists who say the distinction between past, present and future is only an illusion
The behaviour of particles in Quantum Mechanics follows statistical patterns, making it difficult to measure time.
Gisin claims that when time is measured using intuitionist maths, new information is created
Gisin implies that numbers with infinitely many digits are not realistic or meaningful in physics
cl_2023_pc_6_q_9.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
Gisin’s argument challenges the conventional view of physics that assumes the existence of real numbers with infinitely many digits. He claims that such numbers are unrealistic and do not represent the physical world accurately. So 4 is correct

Wrong Answers:
1) In the first para - The passage says - For us believing physicists,” Einstein wrote in 1955, weeks before his death, “the distinction between past, present and future is only a stubbornly persistent illusion”.
This means that for the category of those physicists, the distinction is an illusion, but it doesn’t mean that - As per Einstein, believing in those physicists is an illusion. In other words, Einstein doesn’t think that believing in those physicists is illusion. So, 1 is wrong.
2) The passage says – “Mysteriously, individual measurement outcomes are random and unpredictable, even as particle behaviour collectively follows statistical patterns.”
The mysteriousness & difficulty is in the outcomes being random and unpredictable. Not that because of the statistical patterns it is difficult. So, 2 is wrong.
3) The passage says – “When intuitionist math is used to describe the evolution of physical systems, it makes clear, according to Gisin, that “time really passes and new information is created”.
It means when the intuitionist math describes the evolution of physical systems, time passes and then new information is created”. Not that when time is measured using intuitionist maths, new information is created. So, 3 is wrong.

cl_2023_pc_6_q_11.question_text: From the passage, we can infer that the author uses Albert Einstein’s theory of relativity for all of the following reasons EXCEPT:

To illustrate that the present does not appear in physics laws.
To demonstrate the concept of time as an illusion
To describe that there is no distinction across time divisions
cl_2023_pc_6_q_11.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3

The author uses Einstein's theory of relativity to show that time is not a fundamental property of the universe, but a subjective experience that depends on the observer's state of motion and position in the space-time continuum. According to relativity, there is no absolute present or simultaneity, and events that appear to happen at the same time for one observer may happen at different times for another. This implies that time is not a fixed reality, but an illusion created by our perception.

Wrong Answers:
1) To elaborate how space-time continuum is created - This is incorrect. The author does not explain how the space-time continuum is created, but rather how it works and what it implies for the nature of time. The author does not mention the origin or cause of the space-time continuum, but only describes its properties and consequences.
2) To illustrate that the present does not appear in physics laws - This is incorrect. The author does not use Einstein's theory of relativity to illustrate that the present does not appear in physics laws, but rather that the present is not a universal concept that applies to all observers. The author does not claim that physics laws do not account for the present, but rather that they show that the present is relative and subjective.
4) To describe that there is no distinction across time divisions - This is incorrect. The author does not use Einstein's theory of relativity to describe that there is no distinction across time divisions, but rather that there is no objective distinction between past, present and future. The author does not deny that there are different ways of dividing time, such as seconds, minutes, hours, days, etc., but rather that these divisions are arbitrary and do not reflect the true nature of time.

The exact state of a particle cannot be accounted for in Quantum Mechanics.
Mathematics is not a perfect language to describe nature

The statement it is based on says that individual measurement outcomes are random and unpredictable, but it does not say that the exact state of a particle cannot be accounted for in quantum mechanics. Wrong Answers:

4) This is a correct inference that follows from the statement that numbers are finite and limited in their precision, and that nature is inherently imprecise and unpredictable. Directions for questions 13 to 16: Study the following information and answer the questions that follow:

In contrast to the asceticism, fideism, and authoritarianism of the middle ages, the educational methods of the Renaissance were based on new theories of human nature and cognitive methods. Specifically, the following points were made:

First, they defended the spirit of liberty and opposed authoritarianism and corporal punishment. The ideal state of life, according to Rabelais, is one in which everything obeys one's own will and desire rather than law, regulation, and rule. Erasmus emphasised that individuals had the right to free thought. He proposed that teachers should not allow students to rely on authority but rather their own judgement. Vittorino concurred with Plato's theory that free people could not be instructed using harsh and coercive methods. Vives demanded that teachers show children respect. Montaigne valued tearless instruction. According to him, harsh punishment served no purpose and only served to destroy students' motivation to learn.

Second, the educational and philosophical perspectives of this era emphasised both the mental and physical development of children. The educators of the Renaissance realised that whether or not to adhere to children's developmental characteristics was the determining factor that led directly to educational success. Vergerio argued that learning subjects should be tailored to the interests and ages of students. Erasmus recognised the diversity of students. Depending on the personalities of the students, he believed that different teaching strategies are necessary. Rabelais advocated that children be inspired and respected. He believed that an effective teaching method was one that could make the entire teaching course enjoyable. Similar to Erasmus, Montaigne advocated teaching children to the best of their ability. Among them, Vives has a thorough understanding of child psychology. He attempted to base education on psychology, and his perspective on children's minds reflected the modern spirit.

Thirdly, new ways of comprehending things were incorporated into the humanistic educational philosophy. Rabelais valued the techniques of observing, conversing, playing games, studying in another location or abroad, etc. Other educators concurred with these practises at the time. Vives and Bacon criticised the scholastic approach in depth. According to Vives, scholasticism was characterised by tedious reasoning based on personal experience. This was the genesis of academic pedantry. And the proper technique was induction. He held sensory experiences in high regard and viewed them as the beginning of intellectual activities. Bacon demonstrated the inductive method implied by the height of philosophy. He refused to acknowledge the subjectivity, blindness, authority, and fuzziness of certain things. He proposed combining perceptual and conceptual knowledge, employing rational ability to divide and organise material, and deducing a scientific conclusion. This philosophical understanding served as the solid foundation for the development of new teaching theory, upon which Comenius based his famous intuitive teaching principle.

Fourthly, humanistic educators focused more on training ability than pedantic learning styles. In this regard, Rabelais argued that knowledge without comprehension was soulless garbage. Montaigne believed that both ability and knowledge were essential for education, with ability being more crucial. According to him, the purpose of education was to develop students' skills through knowledge. According to Bacon, intelligence must be trained alongside will and body. He believed that not only did character improve as a result of education, but so did skill. Montaigne and Bacon emphasised the importance of practical knowledge.

options: Vives claimed that the hallmark of scholasticism was a vicarious experiential reasoning. This was the beginning of academic teaching.

cl_2023_pc_6_q_19.question_text: Most people who write about procrastination write about how to cure it. But this is, strictly speaking, impossible. There are an infinite number of things you could be doing. No matter what you work on, you're not working on everything else. So, the question is not how to avoid procrastination, but how to procrastinate well. There are three variants of procrastination, depending on what you do instead of working on something: you could work on (a) nothing, (b) something less important, or (c) something more important. That last type, I'd argue, is good procrastination. That's the "absent-minded professor," who forgets to shave, or eat, or even perhaps look where he's going while he's thinking about some interesting question. His mind is absent from the everyday world because it's hard at work in another. That's the sense in which the most impressive people I know are all procrastinators. They're type-C procrastinators: they put off working on small stuff to work on big stuff.
options: The passage highlights the importance of curing procrastination by focusing on prioritizing tasks of higher significance instead of engaging in lower importance activities or doing nothing at all.

According to the passage, the key to overcoming procrastination lies in efficiently managing time and avoiding tasks of lesser importance, ensuring that one's focus is solely on the most significant tasks at hand.

The passage argues that curing procrastination is indeed possible by adopting strategies that help individuals effectively balance their time between important and less important tasks, thereby maximizing productivity.

The passage emphasizes the idea that rather than seeking to eliminate procrastination, the key lies in mastering the art of productive procrastination by prioritizing significant tasks over trivial ones or inactivity.
cl_2023_pc_6_q_19.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
Your Answer : 4
Option 4 is the correct summary because it accurately captures the main idea of the passage, which is that rather than trying to eliminate procrastination, the focus should be on practicing productive procrastination by prioritizing important tasks over trivial ones or doing nothing at all. The passage suggests that individuals who engage in type-C procrastination, where they postpone smaller tasks to work on more significant ones, exhibit impressive productivity.

Wrong Answers:
Option 1 incorrectly suggests that the passage highlights the importance of curing procrastination. However, the passage explicitly states that curing procrastination is impossible and focuses on how to procrastinate well instead.
Option 2 suggests that the key to overcoming procrastination lies in efficient time management and avoiding tasks of lesser importance. While time management is indirectly mentioned in the passage, the main focus is on the concept of productive procrastination rather than overcoming procrastination entirely.
Option 3 incorrectly states that the passage argues that curing procrastination is possible by balancing time between important and less important tasks. In contrast, the passage argues that curing procrastination is impossible and instead suggests embracing productive procrastination by focusing on significant tasks. Balancing tasks is not the primary argument presented in the passage.

cl_2023_pc_6_q_22.question_text: Contrary to what we often hear, people didn’t think the Earth was flat right up until Columbus sailed to the Americas. In ancient Greece, the philosophers Pythagoras and Parmenides had already recognised that the Earth was spherical. Aristotle pointed out that you could see some stars in Egypt and Cyprus that were not visible at more northerly latitudes, and also that the Earth casts a curved shadow on the moon during a lunar eclipse. The Earth, he concluded with impeccable logic, must be round. The flat-Earth view was dismissed as simply ridiculous – until very recently, with the resurgence of apparently serious flat-Earthism on the internet. Many ideas have been brilliantly upgraded or repurposed for the modern age, and their revival seems newly compelling. Some ideas from the past, on the other hand, are just dead wrong and really should have been left to rot. And their existence is a big problem for our normal assumptions about how the marketplace of ideas operates.

The passage highlights the long-standing awareness of Earth's spherical shape, questions the recent rise of flat-Earthism, and emphasizes the need to reassess the functioning of the marketplace of ideas.

(iii) No project is under progress on day 9 only i.e., the projects that began before 9th have finished, and a new one is yet to start. Except one project, all projects have a duration of more than 5 days.
(iv) Project 2 starts on the 3rd day whereas project 3 ends on the 12th day. Only one of the projects starts on the same day on which the previous project ends.
(v) Everyone, except B and F, work on at least 2 successive projects. B works on a total of 3 projects, out of which no two are successive. In total, there are 3 people involved in 3 successive projects out of the 5. However, these team members may or may not be involved in the same set of projects.

Person D works on the first two projects only.

Also, no two persons donated the same percentage in the same month. The amount of money involved in each month for all cases was a natural number.

(i) The amount donated by Akash in the month of March was 1/10th of the remaining amount with Chitra after her donation in that month.

(iii) Chitra started with a 50% donation.

(v) Boman’s donation in February was Rs. 90 less than 10 times the number of days in that month.

cl_2023_pc_6_q_37.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 1
Your Answer : 1


Boman donated Rs. 200 in the month of February. Total savings is Rs. 2,500.
Hence, required percentage = 200/2500 × 100 = 8%.

A, B, C, D, E, F, and G are seven weightlifters. Each of them takes a day off from the gym on one of the different days of the week, which starts from Monday and ends on Sunday. Additionally, each weightlifter has a different height, with measurements of 165 cm, 169 cm, 170 cm, 172 cm, 174 cm, 176 cm, and 179 cm. They also have different weights, which are 60 kg, 64 kg, 65 kg, 68 kg, 70 kg, 72 kg, and 75 kg. The heights and weights may not correspond to each other in the same order.
The further information is also known that:
(i) The weight difference between the weightlifters who take a day off from gym on Monday and Friday is 1 kg.
(ii) A is 169 cm tall and took a day off from gym on Thursday. F is 179 cm tall.
(iii) C weighs 68 kg and he takes a day off from gym on a day after Thursday. There are only two weightlifters who take a day off from gym between C and D.
(iv) G is 174 cm tall and weighs 2 kg less than D, who takes a day off from gym immediately after B.
(v) E takes a day off from gym immediately before the individual who is 170 cm tall and has the lowest weight. The person who is 165 cm tall takes a day off from gym on Monday.
cl_2023_pc_6_q_41.question_text: What can be the maximum difference in weights (in kg) between the tallest weightlifter and one who is 172 cm tall?
cl_2023_pc_6_q_41.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
Your Answer : 4


Required maximum difference = 72 – 64 = 8 kg.

cl_2023_pc_6_q_49.question_text: The sum of all the distinct five-digit numbers that can be obtained by rearranging the digits in 12321, including itself, is
cl_2023_pc_6_q_49.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
Your Answer : 3
The total number of possible rearrangements of the number 12321 = 5!/2!.2! = 30
The total sum of digits of the units place in all the 30 arrangements combined = 1(12) + 2(12) + 3(6) = 54
Similarly, the sum of digits in ten’s place, hundred’s place, thousand’s place and ten thousand’s place will also be 54.
Hence, the sum of all 30 rearrangements = 54(11111) = 599994.

cl_2023_pc_6_q_50.question_text: Let ‘m’ be any integer such that the quadratic equation 2x2 + mx + 7 = 0 has no real roots and the quadratic equation x2 + (m – 3)x + 2 = 0 has two distinct real roots for x. Then, the number of possible values of ‘m’ is
cl_2023_pc_6_q_50.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2
Your Answer : 2
Since the equation 2x2 + mx + 7 = 0 has no real roots,
m2 < 4 × 2 × 7
Or, m2 < 56
Since m is an integer, the possible values of m are (–7, –6, …, 0, 1, …, 6, 7).
Since the equation x2 + (m – 3)x + 2 = 0 has distinct and real roots,
(m – 3)2 > 4 × 1 × 2
Or, (m – 3)2 > 8
Since (m – 3) is an integer, the possible values of (m – 3) are (…, –4, –3, –2, –1, 0, 6, 7, …)
So possible values of m are (–7, –6, –5, –4, –3, – 2, –1, 0, 6, 7).
Hence, m can take 10 possible values.

cl_2023_pc_6_q_52.question_text: If for every even natural number ‘n’, 46n + 1 + x and 412n – x are divisible by 5, then find the number of values of x, where x lies between –8 and 10.
cl_2023_pc_6_q_52.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
Your Answer : 3
46n + 1 represents an odd power of 4 (and hence would end in 4) = 5M – 1.
Similarly, 412n represents an even power of 4 (and hence would end in 6) = 5K + 1.
So (x – 1) and (–x + 1) must be divisible by 5.
So the values of x that satisfy this condition are: x = –4, 1 and 6.

cl_2023_pc_6_q_54.question_text: N soldiers are divided into groups of 8 or 12 or 15 or 20 each, exactly 6 always being left out. However, if they are divided into groups of 9 each, no one is left out. If N is less than 800, then the maximum number of groups of 15 each that can be made out of N soldiers?
cl_2023_pc_6_q_54.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 32
N < 800
N leaves a remainder of 6 when divided by 8, 12, 15, or 20.
N leaves a remainder of 6 when divided by LCM (8, 12, 15, 20) or 120.
So N = 120x + 6, x = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 or 6
At x = 1, N = 120x + 6 = 126 is a multiple of 9.
At x = 4, N = 120x + 6 is a multiple of 9.
So N = 120(4) + 6 = 486
Since 486 is greater than 126.
So 486 = 15(32) + 6
Hence, when we divide these 486 soldiers into groups of 15, we get 32 groups.

Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
Your Answer : 4
In a group of 500 people, 300 and 200 are women and men respectively. 120 women are under 30 years old and 100 men are under 30 years old.
220 × 0.4 = 88 people prefer coffee.
Hence, required percentage = 88/500 × 100 = 17.6%.
